chunk_summary: Discharge planning for SAM involves caregiver training, structured prescriptions, and follow-up to prevent relapse, ensure safe feeding, and monitor recovery progress.
learning_objectives: Understand the components of a discharge counseling checklist for SAM management.
Learn how to write a structured outpatient prescription for therapeutic food and medications.
Recognize danger signs requiring immediate medical attention post-discharge.
Implement follow-up protocols to reduce defaulting and ensure program quality.
key_facts: Caregiver demonstration of feed preparation, storage, and administration is mandatory before discharge.
Outpatient prescription must specify therapeutic food (e.g., RUTF sachets/day), micronutrient schedules, and next visit date within 7 days.
Danger signs include poor intake, repeated vomiting, fever, hypothermia, fast breathing, convulsions, lethargy, and worsening edema.
High-dose vitamin A administration should be documented to avoid duplication.
Missed visits require tracing within 48–72 hours by designated personnel (e.g., ASHA/AWW/ANM).
reference_quote: Discharge planning in SAM is a handover of risk, not an endpoint. A discharge counseling checklist and a structured outpatient prescription reduce relapse, defaulting, and unsafe home feeding. (National Health Mission, Participant manual for facility-based care of severe acute malnutrition)
document_key_topics: Outpatient prescription template for therapeutic food and medications
Caregiver training and danger sign recognition
Follow-up protocols to ensure program quality
